stem: Evaluate the infinite geometric series \( \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} ([[a]])\left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right)^{k} \), which has first term \( ([[a]]) \) and common ratio \( \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \).
solution: For \( |r| < 1 \), \( \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} a r^{k} = \dfrac{a}{1-r} \). Here \( a = [[a]] \) and \( r = \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \), so the sum is \( \dfrac{[[a]]}{1 - \frac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \dfrac{a}{1+r} \); the geometric-sum formula has \( 1-r \) in the denominator.
This multiplies by \( (1-r) \) instead of dividing by it.
This drops the first term \( a \), using \( \dfrac{1}{1-r} \).
This is the sum starting at \( k=1 \) (first term \( ar \)), i.e. \( \dfrac{ar}{1-r} \).
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.

stem: The repeating decimal \( 0.\overline{[[block]]} \) (the two-digit block \( [[block]] \) repeats forever) can be written as an infinite geometric series with first term \( \dfrac{[[block]]}{100} \) and common ratio \( \dfrac{1}{100} \). What is \( 0.\overline{[[block]]} \) as a fraction in lowest terms?
solution: With first term \( \dfrac{[[block]]}{100} \) and ratio \( \dfrac{1}{100} \), the sum is \( \dfrac{[[block]]/100}{1-1/100} = \dfrac{[[block]]}{99} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses denominator \( 90 \), a common slip when mixing a repeating and non-repeating part.
This treats the block as if only a single digit repeats, using denominator \( 9 \).
This uses denominator \( 999 \), as if the block had three digits.
This drops the \( -1 \) and just uses the first term's own denominator \( 100 \).
This is off by one from the correct fraction.
This is off by one from the correct fraction.

stem: A ball is dropped from a height of \( [[h]] \) meters. Each time it hits the ground, it rebounds to \( \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \) of the height it just fell from, bouncing forever. What is the total distance the ball travels (down and up, summed over all bounces)?
solution: After the initial drop of \( [[h]] \), the ball travels up and down \( 2[[h]]r \), then \( 2[[h]]r^{2} \), and so on, with \( r=\frac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \). The total is \( [[h]] + \dfrac{2[[h]]r}{1-r} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the initial drop is not doubled, treating the whole motion as a single geometric series without the leading \( [[h]] \).
This omits both the initial drop term and the factor of \( 2 \) for up-and-down travel.
This forgets the factor of \( 2 \): each bounce after the first involves both a rise and a fall.
This doubles the initial drop itself instead of only the bounce distances.
This is off by one from the correct total distance.
This is off by one from the correct total distance.

stem: An infinite geometric series with \( |r| < 1 \) has first term \( [[a]] \) and sum \( [[S]] \). What is the common ratio \( r \)?
solution: From \( S = \dfrac{a}{1-r} \), solve for \( r \): \( r = 1 - \dfrac{a}{S} = 1 - \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[S]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports \( a/S \) itself, not \( 1 - a/S \).
This inverts the ratio \( a/S \).
This subtracts \( a \) from \( S \) directly instead of dividing first.
This adds instead of subtracts \( a/S \) from \( 1 \).
This is off by one from the correct ratio.
This is off by one from the correct ratio.

stem: An infinite geometric series with common ratio \( r = \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \) has sum \( [[S]] \). What is its first term?
solution: From \( S = \dfrac{a}{1-r} \), the first term is \( a = S(1-r) = [[S]]\left(1 - \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides by \( (1-r) \) instead of multiplying by it.
This multiplies \( S \) by \( r \) instead of by \( 1-r \).
This subtracts the ratio from \( S \) directly instead of forming \( S(1-r) \).
This uses \( 1+r \) instead of \( 1-r \).
This is off by one from the correct first term.
This is off by one from the correct first term.
